per_turn_leaks.student_message: Eight to ten times a day is a dramatic increase. With that frequency, are the symptoms disrupting your sleep? Do you wake up at night needing to go?
per_turn_leaks.patient_response: To be honest, yeah... I do wake up in the middle of the night sometimes to use the bathroom, but it's not every single night. Maybe 2-3 times a week or so? It's definitely been an adjustment, and I'm usually exhausted when I go back to bed after getting up.

per_turn_leaks.student_message: Nocturnal diarrhea is a red flag — it suggests an organic rather than functional cause. With that many bowel movements, do you feel like you're fully emptying each time, or is there a sensation of incomplete evacuation?
per_turn_leaks.patient_response: Yeah... I'd say it's usually about 75% of the time that I don't feel like I'm completely done after using the bathroom. It's like my body is just constantly trying to get rid of something, you know? And sometimes it feels like there's still a little bit left behind, but other times it's not as bad...

per_turn_leaks.student_message: Tenesmus with bloody diarrhea points strongly toward an inflammatory process in the rectum and colon. With this level of GI inflammation, the body often responds systemically. Have you had any fevers or chills?
per_turn_leaks.patient_response: Yeah, I've had a low-grade fever a few times a week. It's not usually anything too high, just around 98.6 to 99 degrees Fahrenheit, but it's been happening consistently enough that I've noticed it. And yeah, sometimes I feel really cold or clammy, even when the temperature outside is pretty normal.

per_turn_leaks.student_message: Low-grade fevers fit the pattern. Inflammatory bowel disease often has extraintestinal manifestations — the same inflammatory process can affect joints. Have you noticed any joint pain or stiffness?
per_turn_leaks.patient_response: Yeah, actually I have been experiencing some achy pain in my knees and ankles, but it's not like a sharp pain, more of a dull ache. It comes and goes, but it's been bothering me pretty regularly over the past few weeks. And to be honest, it seems like it gets worse when I take ibuprofen for it - which is weird because you'd think that would help, right?

per_turn_leaks.student_message: Knee and ankle pain — those are classic large joint manifestations of IBD. Are you taking anything for the joint pain? Any over-the-counter medications?
per_turn_leaks.patient_response: Yeah, like I mentioned earlier, I take ibuprofen 2-3 times a week for the joint pain, but it seems to make my abdominal symptoms worse. Other than that, I'm not on any other medications besides loperamide for the diarrhea, which hasn't really helped much anyway.

per_turn_leaks.student_message: Ibuprofen is important to note — NSAIDs can actually worsen inflammatory bowel disease. That might be contributing to your flare. With bloody diarrhea eight to ten times a day for three weeks, you must be losing weight. Have you noticed any changes on the scale?
per_turn_leaks.patient_response: Yeah, I have lost some weight... probably around 4 or 5 kilos (9-11 pounds) over the past few weeks. I've been tracking my weight at home because it's hard to tell when you're eating irregularly and not feeling well, but yeah, I'm definitely down a bit from where I was before all this started.

per_turn_leaks.student_message: Four kilos in three weeks is significant — that's consistent with the severity of your symptoms. You mentioned you work long hours at a startup. That kind of stress can trigger and worsen GI inflammation. How are you coping with all of this?
per_turn_leaks.patient_response: Honestly, it's been really tough. I've been trying to power through, but it's affecting my work performance and overall well-being. I'm usually a pretty high-functioning person, but lately, I just feel exhausted all the time. And with the stress from work and these symptoms, I've actually started drinking more beer than usual to try and unwind at night - maybe 4-5 beers instead of 2-3. It's not ideal, but it's been my way of coping.
